coredata.dc:description: Designing efficient and stable electro-catalysts for non-precious metal oxygen evolution reactions (OER) is critical to solve the bottleneck of water splitting. The NiFe2O4/NCQDs composite electrode was fabricated by combining nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) with NiFe2O4 spinel via a hydrothermal method, which showed brilliant OER catalytic performance by modifying on nickel foams (NF). The composites had petal-like structures, and the NiFe2O4 was epitaxially grown on the NF substrates with exposed sites, resulting in enhanced electron transport. The fluorescence quenching of NCQDs was accompanied by binding NCQDs with NiFe2O4, which indicated the energy barrier was greatly reduced during the electron transport as well as electro-catalysis. The higher intrinsic charge transfer was achieved, and the doping combination having a higher conductivity was confirmed. The electrochemical activity of NiFe2O4/NCQDs electrode in OER reaction was evaluated under alkaline conditions. The addition of NCQDs effectively enhanced the OER activity, and over-potentials were decreased from 330 mV to 270 mV at 10 mA cm−2 with Tafel slope lower (66 mV/dec) than counterparts. The NiFe2O4/NCQDs displayed long-term catalytic stability at 1.47 V. Therefore, a facile method for preparing NiFe2O4/NCQDs was developed which had characteristics of low cost, simple process and excellent OER activity.

(a) XRD patterns of NiFe2O4/NCQDs and bare NiFe2O4, (b) the full XPS profiles of NiFe2O4/NCQDs-10 and NiFe2O4, and (c) high-resolution XPS profiles of NiFe2O4/NCQDs-10 showing bands of Ni 2p, (d) Fe 2p, (e) C 1 s, and (f) O 1 s and fitted curves.

(a) PL spectra of NCQDs before and after combining with NiFe2O4 (λex = 365 nm). The insert showed the ratio of D band to G band in the prepared NiFe2O4/NCQDs.

(a) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of (a) NiFe2O4/NCQDs-10 and (b) NiFe2O4, and insets were the corresponding BJH pore size distributions; (c) TG and DTG curves for as-prepared NiFe2O4/NCQDs-10 spinel precursor; and (d) DSC curves of NiFe2O4/NCQDs-10 and NiFe2O4 spinel nanoparticles within the temperature range of 25–500 °C at a ramping rate of 10 °C/min.

Ohm means ‘Ω’.

(a) The electrochemical double-layer capacitances of different catalysts, (b) corresponding roughness factor and electrochemical surface area of samples, (c) polarization curves of NiFe2O4/NCQDs-10 before and after 1000 CV cycles, and (d) theoretical (red line) and experimental oxygen production (blue points) using NiFe2O4/NCQDs-10 in 1.0 M NaOH alkaline solution.

head.ce:title: NiFe-spinel oxides with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for enhanced oxygen evolution activity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing efficient and stable electro-catalysts for non-precious metal oxygen evolution reactions (OER) is critical to solve the bottleneck of water splitting. The NiFe2O4/NCQDs composite electrode was fabricated by combining nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) with NiFe2O4 spinel via a hydrothermal method, which showed brilliant OER catalytic performance by modifying on nickel foams (NF). The composites had petal-like structures, and the NiFe2O4 was epitaxially grown on the NF substrates with exposed sites, resulting in enhanced electron transport. The fluorescence quenching of NCQDs was accompanied by binding NCQDs with NiFe2O4, which indicated the energy barrier was greatly reduced during the electron transport as well as electro-catalysis. The higher intrinsic charge transfer was achieved, and the doping combination having a higher conductivity was confirmed. The electrochemical activity of NiFe2O4/NCQDs electrode in OER reaction was evaluated under alkaline conditions. The addition of NCQDs effectively enhanced the OER activity, and over-potentials were decreased from 330 mV to 270 mV at 10 mA cm−2 with Tafel slope lower (66 mV/dec) than counterparts. The NiFe2O4/NCQDs displayed long-term catalytic stability at 1.47 V. Therefore, a facile method for preparing NiFe2O4/NCQDs was developed which had characteristics of low cost, simple process and excellent OER activity.

ce:section.ce:para: The exploitation of green energy has gradually drawn public concern due to the decreased traditional energy and serious pollution of fossil fuels combustion to the environment [1]. Electrochemical water splitting was recognized as one of the most practical and efficient route to produce clean energy (H2), and has been attracted extensive attention [2]. The electricity-driven water splitting consists of two half reactions, i.e., the cathode two-electron reaction (hydrogen evolution reaction, HER) and anode four-electron reaction (oxygen evolution reaction, OER) [3]. The OER is a four-electron reaction process with a higher activation barrier, and requires more energy which makes the actual potential much higher than the theoretical voltage. It is necessary to develop high-performance catalysts to improve the OER reaction efficiency [4,5]. The most effective electro-catalysts are precious metal-based materials such as RuO2 and IrO2 which were employed to facilitate OERs. Nevertheless, the large-scale application of such noble metal catalysts was limited due to the scarcity of resources and high costs.

The highly efficient non-noble metals (Fe, Ni) and their corresponding oxides have been extensively applied as electrochemical catalysts which have abundant oxygen-vacancies in crystallite cells, good stability and low cost, etc [6,7]. Generally, the spinel oxides of AB2O4 (A, B = metal) indicate superior electro-catalytic activity than mono-metal oxides, such as oxides of Fe, Ni, Co, Mn, and have been validated as excellent electro-catalytic activity, especially in alkaline solutions [8]. Of numerous spinel oxides, the nickel ferrite NiFe2O4 is one of the most effective composite oxides because it has many attractive advantages, such as lattice vacancies, tunable valence states, brilliant chemical/thermal stability and low cost. Mahala et. al. [9] reported a synthetic method for preparing nickel–iron spinel using a wet-chemical route, and the obtained NiFe2O4 was found to require 1.69 V vs. RHE to afford 10 mA cm−2 current density. Liu et. al. [10] published mesoporous NiFe2O4 spinel by thermal decomposing from NiFe-coordination polymers, and the over-potential was 342 mV for OER (at 10 mA cm−2). Kim et. al. [11] have prepared CoFe2O4 based on a metal–organic decomposition (MOD) followed by loading CoFe2O4 onto carbon fibers, and the over-potential was 359 mV at 10 mA cm−2. However, the spinel oxides have an inherent weakness in electrochemical process because of their low conductivity, which significantly limits kinetics of electron transfer [12]. To address this obstacle, strategies were done to combine NiFe2O4 with conductive substrates to improve conductivity and electro-oxidation, and to facilitate charge transfer in the integrated systems [13,14]. After gradual research and development, various approaches have been developed [15,16]. Li et. al. [17] synthesized spinel type NiFe2O4 nanoparticles on multi-walled carbon nanotubes, and the electrical conductivity was found to be enhanced enormously. Fang and co-workers [18] employed Prussian blue (CoFe-PBA) and CoFe2O4 particles on the graphene oxide (RGO) aerogel to promote OER efficiency. Moreover, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) which have rich functional groups similar to hydroxyls and carboxyls can provide favorable sites for electro-active centers, and consequently these dots would enhance electrical performances [19,20]. The nitrogen-doping strategy was further noted to adjust the charge density of conductive sites and ability to adsorb the active species [21], which might improve the electro-catalysis. Although the progresses have been made, an integral growth of spinel oxides with facile recombination at the interface for multiple component materials are still to be sought out to further improve the OER performance.

We here developed an in-situ hydrothermal and a followed calcination step to fabricate composite particles of NiFe2O4 and NCQDs, and the composite particles were grown on the self-supporting Ni foams (NiFe2O4/NCQDs). Variations of optical features of NCQDs were investigated along with combined NiFe2O4, and the fluorescence were decreased which was accompanied by slight wavelength shifts [22,23]. Reduced band gaps were confirmed. Facile excitation for electrons was obtained to transmit between valence and conduction bands [24,25]. Batches of experiments showed that higher carrier concentrations were achieved. When the NiFe2O4 particles were combined with different contents of NCQDs, the electro-catalytic performance was improved to different extents. Compared to the bare NiFe2O4, the composite NiFe2O4/NCQDs electrode displayed lower over-potential down to 270 mV at 10 mA cm−2 in 1.0 M KOH electrolyte solution. Meanwhile, the Tafel slope of NiFe2O4/NCQDs was ∼ 66 mV/dec,which was lower than the NiFe2O4 (82 mV/dec), indicating a different pathway for kinetics. Long-term stability of NiFe2O4/NCQDs was confirmed at an applied voltage of 1.47 V, and no significant decrease in activity was observed. The results showed that the electronic structure optimization of NiFe2O4/NCQDs electrode enhanced the electron transport, thus improved the electrocatalytic performance.

The micro-structural characteristics of NiFe2O4/NCQDs were investigated by the scanning electron microscope (SEM). From the SEM images (Fig. 1 a and Fig. S1 in the supporting information, SI), it could be seen that NiFe2O4/NCQDs nanoflakes were grown on the Ni foams. Here, the sample NiFe2O4/NCQDs-10 was studied for a detailed characterization if no specifically noted which means the dosage of NCQDs was prepared by adding 10 mL (∼1.0 mmol/L) to the NiFe2O4 LDH dispersion (see experimental procedures in the SI). As shown in Fig. 1b, the low-magnification SEM image of NiFe2O4/NCQDs exhibited porous structures assembled into flower petals, and materials appeared as some irregular micro-flakes with various sizes. Based on the higher magnification image (Fig. 1c), every petal was connected with each other. It was indicated that the open space between porous structures and rough surfaces with voids would facilitate mass transfer between ions, thereby improved the Faradic reaction efficiency. Although there was some morphological changes when more quantum dots were incorporated into the lamellar NiFe2O4, petals of spinel were well remained (see SEM images of bare sample NiFe2O4, NiFe2O4/NCQDs-20 and NiFe2O4/NCQDs-40 in Fig. S1). The transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1d) revealed that the NiFe2O4 nanoparticles and NCQDs were successfully bonded, and the composite took shape of petals, large void spaces were clearly discerned among flower petals. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1e) of petals further revealed the fringe spacing of 0.250 nm which was consisted with that of NiFe2O4 (311) lattice planes, and the inter-planar spacing of 0.148 nm corresponded to the NiFe2O4 (440) lattice plane. The selected area electronic diffraction (SAED) pattern of the petaloid NiFe2O4/NCQDs (Fig. 1f) suggested that the synthesized petals were polycrystalline but shared lower crystallinity, and the blurred diffraction rings could be indexed to cubic spinel NiFe2O4. Although there were dots appeared on the NiFe2O4, the crystalline lattice of extra diffraction rings was less visible, suggesting amorphous feature of NCQDs. According to the sample color change after loading NCQDs and the subsequent conductivity measurements, we indicated the presence of NCQDs in the composite electrode.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of sample NiFe2O4 and NiFe2O4/NCQDs nanoparticles further confirmed the spinel formation of nickel ferrite. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the diffraction peaks with 2-theta values of 18.42°, 30.30°, 35.70°, 43.38°, 53.82°, 57.38°, and 63.02° can be indexed to the crystal planes (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) of NiFe2O4, respectively. The diffraction peaks could be labeled as inverse spinel structure (PDF#86–2267). No other peaks were identified as in the XRD pattern, indicating samples were of highly pure spinel phase. The composition or stoichiometric ratios of samples at the surface was analyzed by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Both samples NiFe2O4 and NiFe2O4/NCQDs showed similar patterns in their full XPS spectrs in Fig. 2b. The high-resolution XPS diagrams were shown in Fig. 2c-2f. The presence of NiFe2O4 was confirmed by the appearance of Ni 2p, Fe 2p and shakeup satellite peaks (Sat.) [26]. Bands at 855.5 eV and 873.5 eV were assigned to Ni2+, and the remaining two associated bands at 862.2 and 880.3 eV were attributed to shakeup satellite peaks (Fig. 2c) [27]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Fe 2p (Fig. 2d) showed the Fe species were mainly in the + 3 state, and some other were in form of Fe(II) as evidenced by Fig. 2d. Two main bands around 711.0 eV and 724.2 eV were ascribed to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2. The remaining two associated bands at 713.5 eV and 727.1 eV belonged to the shakeup satellite peaks [28–30]. Moreover, the XPS spectrum of O 1 s was deconvoluted into the following three bands (Fig. 2e). The band at 529.35 eV was the typical metal–oxygen bond, band at 531.05 eV was ascribed to the carbon–oxygen double bond (CO), the band located at 532.62 eV corresponded to carbon–oxygen single bond or nitrogen–oxygen bond (CO/NO) [31], and the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2f) was fitted to four peaks corresponding to bands centered at 284.24 eV (CN), 285.68 eV (CC), 287.84 eV (CO) and 288.87 eV (CO). These findings suggest the carbon quantum dots were chemically bonded with NiFe2O4 nano-flakes.

The optical absorption of NCQDs was analyzed by the UV–Vis spectrometer, and the spectra were displayed in the SI (see Fig. S2). The NCQDs displayed UV–Vis absorption peaks at 230 nm and a minor absorption band at 330 nm, which reflected the π → π* transitions from CC and n → π* transitions from CO double bond with presence of surface amino groups. The PL spectra of NCQDs before and after hydrothermal reaction were obtained at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, and the spectrum was displayed in Fig. 3 a. The PL emission spectrum presented a prominent peak at 480 nm that was identified as the short-wave blue light. The sample was also shined by an ultraviolet lamp (265 nm), and the blue-colored photograph of NCQDs was shown as inset in Fig. 3a. This result was consistent with results of PL spectrum [24,32]. Meanwhile, the composite catalysts showed similar PL spectra, but lower intensity as observed. It was observed that the PL intensity was greatly weakened after combining NCQDs with NiFe2O4 [33]. Moreover, the maximum of PL peak was observed to red-shifted after combining with NiFe2O4 petals with NCQDs. Based on these results, photo-generated electrons were efficiently transferred from NiFe2O4 to NCQDs nanoparticles, thereby the PL quenching occurred with the addition of NCQDs [34,35], which was indicated to be able to improve the electro-catalytic activities via electron transferring [36]. Due to the presence of NCQDs NPs, the wavelength position was red-shifted, and the energy became weaker, which leaded to the consequence of the smaller band gap (1.40 → 1.33 eV). The optical properties of samples were further explored by UV–Vis DRS analysis because the powders were grown on NF substrate and were tested as in solid forms. As shown in Fig. S3 (see the SI), samples exhibited good optical absorption effect in the ultraviolet region, reflecting the benefit for generation of photo-induced charges. According to the absorption data obtained, the optical band-gap energy of the sample was calculated according to the Tauc plot model (eq. 1 ∼ 3) [35,37]. The optical band gap of all samples was calculated by the following equation:

, in which α is the absorbance coefficient and n = 2 for direct transition, and n = 0.5 for indirect transition. By extrapolating the linear part of dependence, the relationship between (αhν)0.5 and energy (hν) clearly demonstrated the approximate band gap value of composite materials as shown in Fig. 3b. The estimated values optical band-gaps of NiFe2O4, NiFe2O4/NCQDs-20, NiFe2O4/NCQDs and NiFe2O4/NCQDs-40 were found to be 1.40 eV, 1.35 eV, 1.28 eV and 1.33 eV, respectively. Therefore, lower band gap energies were obtained from different design of composites. Compared with the bare NiFe2O4, the lower band gap of NiFe2O4/NCQDs was indicated to have higher electron mobility in NiFe2O4/NCQDs. The NCQDs doping would certainly enhance the transfer of electrons between valence and conduction bands, thereby improving electronic conductivity of NiFe2O4 with the external substances.

For FT-IR characterization (Fig. 3c and Fig. S4), the stretching vibrations of OH bonds in the interlayer water molecules resulted in a wide peak around 3450 cm−1. Bands observed between 1350 cm−1 and 2950 cm−1 were indexed to the bending vibrations of CN, CO, and CH, respectively. The peak obtained at 690 cm−1 and 480 cm−1 were due to the bending vibrations of M−O. In the case of NiFe2O4 doped with NCQDs, new bonding emerged at 1520 cm−1 corresponding to the CC which reflects the chemical interactions between NCQDs. Compared to the FT-IR spectra of bare NiFe2O4, the shifting of some parental peaks toward higher wavenumbers and some additional peaks moved towards the lower wavenumber, indicating that NCQDs was successfully doped in NiFe2O4 petals. As shown in Fig. 3d and Fig. S5, the Raman characteristic peaks at 690 cm−1 and 480 cm−1 belonged to A1g and F2g(b) modes, and bands at 565 cm−1, 322 cm−1 and 199 cm−1 were assigned to F2g(c), Eg and F2g(a) modes, respectively, which revealed the well presence of NiFe2O4. Compared to the pure phase of NiFe2O4, the Raman spectra (Fig. 3d) of NiFe2O4/NCQDs catalyst showed two significant peaks at 1345 cm−1 and 1550 cm−1 which were due to the D and G bands corresponding to disordered and sp2 bonded carbons, respectively. In the inserted figure of Fig. 3d, ratios of disordered D and crystalline G (ID/IG) were found to be 0.83 in the composite NiFe2O4/NCQDs, which means there were almost half portion of graphite carbon. Nevertheless, the bare NiFe2O4 only had a slight D band, and almost no G band appeared, suggesting the combination of both entities would increase the electron hybridization of graphene to some extent [38]. Previous reports had shown that the active sites and defects were conducive to enhancing catalytic activity [39]. By careful comparison of the Raman spectra, the shifting and broadening of bands were indicated to the recombination of NCQDs and NiFe2O4, and this hybridization would surely enhance the conductivity of substrate of NiFe2O4 petals [40].

The specific surface area and pore-size volume of NiFe2O4/NCQDs and pure NiFe2O4 were derived from the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms as shown in Fig. 4 a and Fig. 4b (insert figure was the pore size distribution). The samples exhibited a classic H4 hysteresis loop, which was owned to their mesoporous structures with narrow porosity. The obtained NiFe2O4/NCQDs showed the largest BET. The specific surface area was 89.417 m2/g with pore volume of 0.145 cm3/g, and the pore size ranged from 2.0 nm to 159.3 nm, as shown in Fig. 4a. For comparison, the N2-adsorption isotherm of bare sample NiFe2O4 also exhibited type IV isotherm but showed less hysteresis loop (Fig. 4b). As shown by the insert figure of Fig. 4b, the BET showing the specific surface area of NiFe2O4 was 12.078 m2/g, and the pore volume was 0.051 cm3/g, so the NiFe2O4/NCQDs exhibited a larger surface area. Certainly, the evidence suggests that the porous structures in NiFe2O4/NCQDs facilitate electron transport and would provide more active catalytic sites for OER reactions [41]. It can be predicted that the electrochemical performance of NiFe2O4/NCQDs was obviously better than that of NiFe2O4 under the same conditions. The effect of wettability of the catalyst on activity of materials was also tested, and the contact angles of NiFe2O4/NCQDs and graphite paper was measured (Fig. S6, see the SI). Notably, a hydrophilic surface was observed on the NiFe2O4/NCQDs heterostructure because of the hierarchical nanostructures. The excellent wettability accelerates detachment of bubbles from the surface of the porous materials and reduces the Ohmic resistance as anticipated [42].

We also carried out the thermal measurements on our prepared samples. Fig. 4c depicted the thermogravimetric/derivative thermogravimetry (TG/DTG) curves of the as-prepared samples without calcination for NiFe2O4/NCQDs and NiFe2O4 (Fig. S7), respectively. To evaluate the thermal behavior of samples, DTA and DSC (differential thermal/differential scanning calorimetry) profiles were provided in Fig. 4d in the main text and Fig. S8 in the SI. Detailed DSC spectroscopy of NiFe2O4/NCQDs and bare NiFe2O4 were presented in Fig. S9 (a) and (b) (see the SI), and the heat absorption and heat release of each process were evaluated. The curves of spinel precursor indicated that the weight loss of samples occurred in two stages. In the case of NiFe2O4/NCQDs, the first stage corresponded to temperature range from normal temperature to 170 °C, and the weight loss obtained at the end of the first process (T≈170 °C) was 9.5 %. There was an endothermic peak, endothermic heat was 0.229 J, which was ascribed to desorption of the crystal water after decomposition. The second stage took place from 170 °C to 350 °C, and a weight loss of 22.7 % was observed up to 350 °C. The exothermic peaks appeared at 280 °C, exothermic heat was 0.375 J, which corresponded to the combustion of NCQDs to form CO2. When the calcination temperature was increased from 350 °C to 700 °C, there was a small exothermic peak at 390 °C, and no huge mass change occurred at this temperature region, indicating no physicochemical reaction occurred in this temperature range [43,44]. The final total burn reduction of NiFe2O4/NQCDs was 32.2 %. Besides, the TG curve of bare NiFe2O4 precursor indicated that the weight loss of samples occurred in two stages. Correspondingly, there were two endothermic peaks, the first endothermic peak was the desorption process of the crystal water from normal temperature to 175 °C (endothermic heat was 0.094 J). Even though the release of crystalline water molecules was not capable of being clearly identified from the TG curve, it can be inferred from the DSC curve that the dehydration process occurred below 165 °C, because of the endothermic peak in this temperature. There was another endothermic process from 270 °C–550 °C, with a procedural endothermic heat of 0.689 J, which corresponded to the endothermic dehydration of the hydroxyl group in the composite. The total calcination weight loss of NiFe2O4 was 24.0 %. The endothermic and exothermic regions in DTA pattern were basically in consistence with the variation region in TG patterns. Herein, subtracting the burn loss of the above we concluded that the doping content of carbon was 8.2 %. The optimal temperature of calcination was determined to be 350 °C.

The electrocatalytic activities of the NiFe2O4 spinel with different addition of NCQDs were evaluated for studying the electrocatalytic performance in OER catalysis. The polarization curves in Fig. 5 a showed that all NiFe2O4 with addition of NCQDs demonstrated higher OER activity than bare NiFe2O4 electrodes. The NiFe2O4/NCQDs exhibited the most excellent activity with over-potential (η) of 270 mV at 10 mA cm−2, whereas samples NiFe2O4/NCQDs-20 and NiFe2O4/NCQDs-40 exhibited over-potential of 310 mV and 300 mV at 10 mA cm−2, respectively. Based on the LSV curves, we found the electrocatalytic activity of NiFe2O4 doped with NCQDs was greatly enhanced. This would provide more active sites for OER, which was also demonstrated in the specific surface area measurements. Generally, the NiFe2O4 was considered to be an excellent electrocatalyst for OER, however if one utilized them alone without effective supports, the poor performance was always obtained due to the poor electron conduction and lack of interactions between the NiFe2O4 particles and substrate of NF. Herein, the NiFe2O4 combined with NCQDs were observed to improve the catalytic activity. Due to the presence of NCQDs NPs, PL quenching took place and the band gap energy decreased, thereby the NCQDs enhanced the transfer of electrons between valence and conduction bands. As shown in Fig. 5b, the activity of composite catalysts for OER were demonstrated by comparing the over-potential at different current densities. At current densities of 10, 30 and 60 mA cm−2, the NiFe2O4/NCQDs showed lower over-potential than other electrodes. The Tafel slope were provided in Fig. 5c. Although there were numerous interpretations of the Tafel slope values. In alkaline solutions OO bond formation kinetics was considered to be the rate-determining step of OER such as O+O → O2 or H2O+O → OOH+H+ + e−. The lower value of NiFe2O4/NCQDs than 88 mV dec-1 of the bare NiFe2O4 can therefore be interpretated as the kinetic rate was obviously increased after doping. The presence of NCQDs on NiFe2O4 resulted in a lower Tafel slope, which increased the surface area in addition to improved the conductivity. The comparative diagrams of over-potentials for different sampels at 10 mA cm−2 and the correspoing Tafel slope values were presented in Fig. S10 (see the SI). As evidenced, the NiFe2O4/NCQDs emerged excellent catalytic activity with an over-potential of 270 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 with corresponding Tafel slope of 66 mV dec-1. For comparison, the bare NiFe2O4 showed over-potential of 330 mV at the same current density with Tafel slope of 82 mV dec-1, which can be seen that the NiFe2O4/NCQDs was superior to other electrodes in terms of over-potential and Tafel slopes. By briefing the literatures (Table 1 ), it was concluded that our designed NiFe2O4/NCQDs electrode exhibited an excellent electrocatalytic performance than the majority of other previously reported NiFe2O4 based catalysts. For example, we observed that the over-potential of S-NiFe2O4/NF was 420 mV [45], which was much higher than NiFe2O4/NCQDs with a similar Tafel slope. These results demonstrated the developed electrode shared a low over-potential electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution reaction. The electrical conductivity of the electrocatalyst was evaluated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) as shown in the SI (Table S1). The Nyquist plots were obtained at an operating voltage of 0.4 V, and the charge transfer resistance (Rct) was calculated, as shown in Fig. 5d. The smaller characteristic semicircle formed in Nyquist plots, the faster the kinetics. NiFe2O4/NCQDs showed Rct value of 11.12 Ω, whereas this value increased for bare NiFe2O4 to 109.8 Ω. It can be seen that semicircles of all the NiFe2O4 combined with NCQDs electrodes were much smaller than that of bare NiFe2O4, This indicated that the NiFe2O4 electrode doped with NCQDs improved the charge transfer kinetics, which can promote the conductivity to be greatly increased. Moreover, the Rct value of the NiFe2O4/NCQDs electrode was the lowest among all electrodes, indicating that the facile synthesis of NiFe2O4/NCQDs electrode guaranteed a excellent interfacial connection to the substrate.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of the fabricated electrodes were demonstrated in Fig. S11 provided in the SI. The double layer capacitances (Cdl) of catalysts were derived by taking CV scan rates from 0.87 to 0.97 V potential vs. RHE as shown in Fig. 6 a, which represented the plot between current densities and scan rates range of 10–50 mV s−1 with slope corresponding to the Cdl (derived from slope of plot of scan rate versus (ja-jc/2), figures provided in Fig. S11). As expected, NiFe2O4/NCQDs electrode outperformed NiFe2O4 with higher Cdl as 4.98 mF cm−2 vs. 0.93 mF cm−2. The Cdl of synthesized electrodes were drawn from cyclic voltammetry slops that were measured at different scan rates (10–50 mV s−1). The derived Cdl and electrochemical surface areas (ECSA) for all electrodes were listed in Table S2. It could be seen that electrodes with added NCQDs were all higher than bare NiFe2O4. In addition, the roughness factors (RFs) and ECSA were calculated based on Cdl of different catalysts in Fig. 6b [56], and the computational formula of RFs and ECSA were as equation (S5, see the SI) and equation (S6), respectively. As shown in Fig. 6b, the ECSA of NiFe2O4/NCQDs catalyst was 124.5 m2 g−1 (NiFe2O4 23.25 m2 g−1), and that of RF was 62.25 (NiFe2O4 11.62), almost five times that of bare NiFe2O4. The NiFe2O4/NCQDs catalyst had the largest specific surface area and the highest roughness, which would expose more active sites and provide strong evidence to our indications. In general, the large ECSA allowed intimate contact with the electrolyte, while providing abundant active site for catalysis, which was undoubtedly the reason for enhanced catalytic activity of NiFe2O4/NCQDs [57]. Due to the difficulty in evaluating the exact surface sites of composite NiFe2O4/NCQDs and the geometric areas because of presence of NCQDs on LDH crystallites, we assumed the surface sites barely come from the geometric area of LDH unit cell, i.e., parameters of a = b = 3.079 Å, c = 22.790 Å, α = β = 90°, γ = 120°, and the volume of hexagonal cell was appointed to 187.11 Å3. Using these unit cell data, the surface sites were roughly determined, and thus the turnover frequency (TOF) curves were obtained. Results were shown in the SI (Fig. S12). Certainly, the TOF values of the NiFe2O4/NCQDs samples were higher than that of bare Ni3Fe LDH sample, indicating that the presence of NCQDs was in favor of enhancing the conversion of water into O2. The continuous cyclic voltammetry of NiFe2O4/NCQDs electrode in the range of 1.0 ∼ 1.8 V (vs. RHE) was tested for 1000 cycles at a scanning rate of 50 mV s−1. As shown in Fig. 6c, the LSV polarization curve showed that there was no obvious loss of current density after 1000 CV cycles. Fascinatingly, the durability of NiFe2O4/NCQDs was tested for 20.0 h at an applied voltage of 1.47 V (Fig. S13), and the results showed an only slight decrease in current density. For checking the stability, we further characterized the NiFe2O4/NCQDs sample by scanning at a higher current of 100 mA cm−2 for 24 h, and no voltage drop was observed, suggesting the material sustain good stability under higher current density (Fig. S14). Meanwhile, the sample morphology was almost unchanged after the stability test by SEM (Fig. S15), indicating that NiFe2O4/NCQDs had relative well stability in alkaline water splitting. Moreover, theoretical and actual oxygen production were calculated and measured. The following formula (4) was used to calculate the theoretical oxygen production:
(4)
n
O 2
=
i × t
4 × e ×
N A
⋯
where the “i” is the current density, i = 50 mA cm−2, “t” is time and “e” is the elementary charge. The actual amounts of generated O2 were confirmed by Hoffmann electrolytic water device (Fig. S16) with PARSTAT 3000A-DX electrochemical workstation with anode of NiFe2O4/NCQDs and cathode of platinum plate [58]. The actual amount of gas generated in the experiment at 50mA cm−2 current density was shown in the Fig. 6d, it can be observed that was in general consistent with theoretical calculation and was detected with Faradaic efficiencies over 95 % for the OER [59].

In conclusion, the composite made from NiFe-spinel oxides and NCQDs was successfully synthesized by a simple hydrothermal procedure. The composite possessed a porous structure of petals, rough surfaces and voids which facilitate ion access to the interface, and these indications were confirmed by SEM, TEM, and BET. analyses. With the addition of NCQDs, PL quenching took place, which was interpreted by the efficient delivery of photo-generated electrons between nano-sheets of NiFe2O4 and NCQDs. This phenomenon resulted in the effective separation of electrons and holes, thus improved electrical conductivity. The NiFe2O4/NCQDs electrode was demonstrated to show excellent electro-catalytic activity, to decrease the over-potential from 330 mV to 270 mV at 10 mA cm−2 current density in 1.0 M KOH, and a Tafel slope of 66 mV/dec was achieved. The enhancement of OER performance can be ascribed to following factors: I) the well-dispersed NCQDs with can provide more active sites for catalytic reaction; II) the large surface area and superior electrical conductivity of NiFe2O4/NCQDs composite could accelerate the effective electron transport efficiency, thereby improving OER performance. This work provided a new method to develop cost-effective novel electro-catalysts to prevent NPs aggregation.
All experimental details are presented in the Supporting Information.
